findings.title: Silent fallback on `TypeError` hides real bugs and may pass wrong `fsize`
findings.detail:
When `make_conditional(request, accept_ranges=True, complete_length=fsize)` raises `TypeError` (indicating an older werkzeug that doesn't support the new signature), the code silently falls back to `rv.make_conditional(request)`. This has two problems:
1. Any *other* `TypeError` raised inside `make_conditional` (e.g. a bug in werkzeug or a wrong type passed) will also be swallowed and silently fall back, masking real errors.
2. On older werkzeug without range-request support, if `fsize` is `None` (file object path), this fallback is correct, but there is no signal to the caller that range requests are not actually supported.
A safer approach is to detect werkzeug version/capability at import time or to check for the specific signature before calling, rather than catching a broad `TypeError`.

findings.title: File leaked when `close_on_416=False` and 416 is raised for file-object input
findings.detail: When `filename_or_fp` is a file object (not a string path), `close_on_416` remains `False`. If `make_conditional` raises `RequestedRangeNotSatisfiable`, the `except` block skips closing the file because `close_on_416 is False`, then re-raises. However the file object that was passed in (or even opened by the caller) is now wrapped but never iterated and never closed. This is an inconsistency: a file opened internally IS closed (correct), but a caller-supplied file object that triggers a 416 is silently leaked. At minimum a comment explaining the reasoning (i.e. caller owns lifetime of passed-in objects) would help; ideally the response object should still be closed properly before re-raising.

findings.title: Missing test for 416 response body and `Content-Range` header
findings.detail: RFC 7233 requires that a 416 (Range Not Satisfiable) response MUST include a `Content-Range: bytes */<complete-length>` header. The test verifies the status code is 416 but does not assert that the `Content-Range` header is present and correct, nor that the response body matches the werkzeug error page. This reduces confidence that the 416 response is fully RFC-compliant.

findings.title: 304 x-sendfile removal also needed for 206 partial-content responses
findings.detail: The code removes the `x-sendfile` header only for 304 responses, but the same reasoning (some servers ignore special status codes for x-sendfile) could apply to 206 partial-content responses. If x-sendfile is in the headers and the response status is 206, the x-sendfile header may be misinterpreted by the front-end server. Consider also popping `x-sendfile` when `rv.status_code == 206`.

findings.title: Test assertion for partial content uses file read after `rv.data` comparison — both correct but fragile
findings.detail: Each test case opens the resource file and reads it independently. Since `index.html` is small and static this is fine, but for `bytes=4-15` the assertion `rv.data == f.read()[4:16]` is correct (end is inclusive in the Range header, exclusive in Python slicing). This is good. However, there is no assertion on the `Content-Range` response header (e.g. `Content-Range: bytes 4-15/20`) to confirm the server properly communicates which range was returned.

findings.title: `close_on_416` flag is an indirect and fragile way to track file ownership
findings.detail: Using a separate boolean `close_on_416` to track whether this function opened the file (and thus owns it) is fragile: if new code paths are added in the future, it is easy to forget to update this flag. A cleaner pattern would be to introduce a local `file_opened_here` variable, or to simply check `isinstance(filename_or_fp, string_types)` again at the point of cleanup, keeping the ownership semantics explicit.

findings.title: Typo in `skipif` reason string
findings.detail: The `reason` string reads `"not implement within werkzeug"` which should be `"not implemented within werkzeug"` (missing 'd').

findings.title: Doc comment says 'upgrade the response stream' — imprecise wording
findings.detail: The new docstring addition says 'this method will try to upgrade the response stream to support range requests'. This phrasing is a bit ambiguous. It would be clearer to say 'this method will respond with a 206 Partial Content response if a valid Range header is present in the request, and will set Accept-Ranges on the response.'

When `make_conditional(request, accept_ranges=True, complete_length=fsize)` raises `TypeError` (indicating an older werkzeug that doesn't support the new signature), the code silently falls back to `rv.make_conditional(request)`. This has two problems:
